Breast Cancer Diagnosis Using Machine Learning¶

Dataset Source¶

The dataset used in this project is the Breast Cancer Wisconsin (Diagnostic) Dataset (WDBC) obtained from the UCI Machine Learning Repository.

Data Description

The dataset contains 569 instances with 30 numerical features derived from digitized images of fine needle aspiration (FNA) of breast masses. The target variable represents tumor diagnosis:

  • M = Malignant

  • B = Benign

This dataset is publicly available and widely used for machine learning research and academic purposes.

Problem Statement¶

Business / Healthcare Context¶

Breast cancer is one of the most common and serious health conditions affecting women worldwide. It occurs when abnormal breast cells grow uncontrollably and form malignant tumors, which can spread to other parts of the body if not detected early. Clinical research has consistently shown that early detection significantly improves survival rates, reduces the need for aggressive treatment, and leads to better long-term patient outcomes.

Modern diagnostic procedures, such as fine needle aspirate (FNA) biopsies, generate a large number of quantitative measurements describing tumor characteristics related to size, shape, texture, and structural irregularities. While these measurements contain valuable diagnostic information, manually interpreting complex patterns across many features can be challenging, time-consuming, and subject to variability across practitioners.

Problem Definition¶

The key challenge addressed in this project is to determine whether supervised machine learning models, trained on structured diagnostic data derived from FNA biopsies, can accurately and reliably distinguish between benign and malignant breast tumors. Improving the accuracy and consistency of tumor classification can support early diagnosis and help prioritize high-risk cases for further clinical evaluation.

Project Objective¶

You are working as a data analyst/data scientist in a healthcare analytics context. Your task is to analyze a publicly available breast cancer diagnostic dataset and develop machine learning classification models that can assist in identifying malignant tumors with high predictive performance.

Specifically, this project aims to:

  • Identify the most influential diagnostic features associated with breast cancer malignancy

  • Build supervised machine learning classification models to predict tumor diagnosis

  • Compare the performance of multiple models using appropriate evaluation metrics

  • Examine whether a reduced set of diagnostic features can maintain reliable predictive accuracy

Expected Outcome¶

The outcome of this project is a set of evaluated and interpretable machine learning models that demonstrate the feasibility of using structured diagnostic data for early breast cancer detection. While the models developed are not intended for direct clinical deployment, they illustrate how analytics and machine learning can serve as decision-support tools to complement clinical expertise.

Scope and Objectives¶

Scope of the Study¶

The scope of this project is limited to the analysis of structured numerical data derived from digitized images of fine needle aspirate (FNA) biopsies. The dataset consists exclusively of quantitative diagnostic features describing tumor size, shape, and texture, with no use of medical images or unstructured clinical notes. The analysis is conducted using a publicly available breast cancer diagnostic dataset and is designed to be completed within an academic capstone timeframe.

Project Objectives¶

The primary objectives of this study are to:

  • Identify diagnostic features that are strongly associated with breast cancer malignancy
  • Develop supervised machine learning models to classify tumors as benign or malignant
  • Compare predictive performance across multiple classification models
  • Explore feature importance to assess whether a reduced subset of features can support reliable prediction performance

Research Focus and Methodological Alignment¶

To address these objectives, the project follows an exploratory and model-driven analytical approach. Feature relevance is examined using correlation analysis and tree-based feature importance methods. Classification performance is evaluated using multiple supervised learning models, and comparative performance is assessed using standard evaluation metrics such as accuracy, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

Rather than formal statistical hypothesis testing, the analysis emphasizes empirical model performance and interpretability, which aligns with common practices in applied machine learning and healthcare analytics.

Import Libraries¶

In [21]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# To tune model, get different metric scores, and split data
from sklearn.metrics import (
    f1_score,
    accuracy_score,
    recall_score,
    precision_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    roc_auc_score,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
)
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score

# To be used for data scaling and one hot encoding
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, OneHotEncoder

# To impute missing values
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn import metrics

# To oversample and undersample data
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

# To do hyperparameter tuning
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV

# To define maximum number of columns to be displayed in a dataframe
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

# To supress scientific notations for a dataframe
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: "%.3f" % x)

# To help with model building
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    AdaBoostClassifier,
    GradientBoostingClassifier,
    RandomForestClassifier,
    BaggingClassifier,
)
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# To suppress scientific notations
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: "%.3f" % x)

# To supress warnings
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

columns = [
    "ID",
    "Diagnosis",  # M = malignant, B = benign
    "radius_mean", "texture_mean", "perimeter_mean", "area_mean", "smoothness_mean",
    "compactness_mean", "concavity_mean", "concave_points_mean", "symmetry_mean", "fractal_dimension_mean",
    "radius_se", "texture_se", "perimeter_se", "area_se", "smoothness_se",
    "compactness_se", "concavity_se", "concave_points_se", "symmetry_se", "fractal_dimension_se",
    "radius_worst", "texture_worst", "perimeter_worst", "area_worst", "smoothness_worst",
    "compactness_worst", "concavity_worst", "concave_points_worst", "symmetry_worst", "fractal_dimension_worst"
]

data.columns = columns

Observations¶

  • The dataset has 569 records and 32 columns. There are no missing values. One column is an ID, one is the target variable (Diagnosis), and the remaining 30 are numeric medical features. I will drop the ID column, encode the Diagnosis column, and perform EDA mainly on selected important features and overall statistics rather than analyzing all 30 individually.

8 rows × 31 columns

Observations (Summary)¶

  • Tumor size–related features such as radius, perimeter, and area show a wide range of values, indicating high variability across patients.
  • Worst-case measurements are consistently higher than their corresponding mean values, which aligns with the medical definition of these features.
  • Several shape-related features, including concavity and concave points, have minimum values of zero, indicating absence of these characteristics in some tumors.
  • Some worst features (especially compactness and concavity) show very high maximum values, suggesting the presence of potential outliers.
  • All numeric features have valid ranges with no missing values, confirming the dataset is clean and suitable for further analysis and modeling.

Overall, the data shows strong variability in tumor size and shape, clear differences between mean and worst measurements, and no missing values, making it well suited for classification modeling.

Observations¶

  • ID has 569 unique values, equal to the number of records, confirming it is a unique identifier and should be excluded from modeling.
  • Diagnosis has only 2 unique values (Malignant and Benign), confirming it is a binary target variable suitable for classification.
  • All numeric feature columns have a high number of unique values (mostly above 400), indicating they are continuous variables, not categorical.
  • Area, perimeter, and radius features show very high uniqueness, suggesting strong variability and good discriminatory potential between classes.
  • No feature shows extremely low uniqueness, indicating there are no constant or near-constant columns that need to be removed.

The unique value analysis confirms that ID is a pure identifier, Diagnosis is a binary target, and all remaining features are continuous with sufficient variability for modeling.”

Univariate Analysis of Selected Clinically Important Features¶

Although the dataset contains 30 numeric features, detailed univariate analysis was performed only on selected clinically important features. Summary statistics and unique value analysis were used to understand the remaining features.

  • radius_mean
  • area_mean
  • concavity_mean
  • radius_worst
  • area_worst
  • concave_points_worst
In [ ]:
selected_features = [
    "radius_mean",
    "area_mean",
    "concavity_mean",
    "radius_worst",
    "area_worst",
    "concave_points_worst"
]
In [ ]:
remaining_features = [
    col for col in df.columns
    if col not in selected_features + ["Diagnosis"]
]
In [ ]:
#Summary statistics for remaining features

Observations (Remaining Features – Summary Statistics & Unique Values)¶

  • All remaining numeric features have 569 non-null observations and a high number of unique values, confirming they are continuous variables with sufficient variability for modeling.
  • Mean-based features such as texture_mean, perimeter_mean, and compactness_mean show moderate to high spread, indicating noticeable differences in tumor texture and shape across samples.
  • Standard-error features (*_se) have smaller magnitudes but relatively high uniqueness, reflecting measurement variability rather than absolute tumor size.
  • Several features, particularly area_se, perimeter_se, and shape-related measures (concavity_se, compactness_se), exhibit large ranges and high maximum values, suggesting the presence of outliers.
  • Worst-case features generally have higher means and wider ranges than their corresponding mean features, reinforcing their importance in capturing extreme tumor characteristics.
  • No feature shows near-zero variance or low cardinality, indicating that no additional features need to be removed based on univariate variability.
  • Overall, the summary statistics confirm that the remaining features are well-distributed, informative, and suitable for downstream bivariate analysis and predictive modeling.

The summary statistics and unique value analysis confirm that all remaining features are continuous, variable, and free from data quality issues, supporting their inclusion in further analysis.”

In [ ]:
# function to create labeled barplots


def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
    """
    Barplot with percentage at the top

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
    n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all levels)
    """

    total = len(data[feature])  # length of the column
    count = data[feature].nunique()
    if n is None:
        plt.figure(figsize=(count + 1, 5))
    else:
        plt.figure(figsize=(n + 1, 5))

    plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
    ax = sns.countplot(
        data=data,
        x=feature,
        palette="Paired",
        order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n].sort_values(),
    )

    for p in ax.patches:
        if perc == True:
            label = "{:.1f}%".format(
                100 * p.get_height() / total
            )  # percentage of each class of the category
        else:
            label = p.get_height()  # count of each level of the category

        x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2  # width of the plot
        y = p.get_height()  # height of the plot

        ax.annotate(
            label,
            (x, y),
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            size=12,
            xytext=(0, 5),
            textcoords="offset points",
        )  # annotate the percentage

    plt.show()  # show the plot
In [ ]:
# function to plot a boxplot and a histogram along the same scale.


def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(12, 7), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (12,7))
    kde: whether to the show density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins, palette="winter"
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram
In [ ]:
# function to plot stacked bar chart

def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
    """
    Print the category counts and plot a stacked bar chart

    data: dataframe
    predictor: independent variable
    target: target variable
    """
    count = data[predictor].nunique()
    sorter = data[target].value_counts().index[-1]
    tab1 = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], margins=True).sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    print(tab1)
    print("-" * 120)
    tab = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], normalize="index").sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    tab.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, figsize=(count + 1, 5))
    plt.legend(
        loc="lower left", frameon=False,
    )
    plt.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1))
    plt.show()
In [ ]:
### Function to plot distributions

def distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, predictor, target):

    fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))

    target_uniq = data[target].unique()

    axs[0, 0].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[0]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[0]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 0],
        color="teal",
    )

    axs[0, 1].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[1]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[1]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 1],
        color="orange",
    )

    axs[1, 0].set_title("Boxplot w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=target, y=predictor, ax=axs[1, 0], palette="gist_rainbow")

    axs[1, 1].set_title("Boxplot (without outliers) w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data,
        x=target,
        y=predictor,
        ax=axs[1, 1],
        showfliers=False,
        palette="gist_rainbow",
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Observations on Diagnosis¶

In [ ]:
# observations on Diagnosis
labeled_barplot(df, "Diagnosis")

Observations:¶

  • There are 357 benign samples compared to 212 malignant samples, indicating that approximately 63% of the diagnoses in this study are non-cancerous.

Observations on radius_mean¶

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(data, "radius_mean", kde=True)

Observations:¶

  • The data is right-skewed (positively skewed), as indicated by the long tail extending toward higher values and the mean (green dashed line/triangle) being greater than the median (solid black line).

  • There are several outliers present on the higher end of the scale (above 25), visible as individual points beyond the right whisker of the box plot.

  • Malignant cases typically exhibit a higher average radius_mean and a broader spread of values compared to Benign cases, as cancerous tumors tend to be larger and more varied in size.

Observations on area_mean¶

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(data, "area_mean", kde=True)

Observations:¶

  • The distribution of area_mean is highly right-skewed (positively skewed), with a significant concentration of values between 200 and 800 and a long tail extending toward values as high as 2,500.

  • There is a clear distinction between classes, as Malignant (M) tumors generally exhibit much larger area_mean values and greater variability compared to Benign (B) tumors, which are tightly clustered at the lower end of the scale.

  • The clear separation in distributions between classes makes area_mean a highly predictive feature, where values exceeding roughly 750 $units^2$ serve as a strong morphological indicator for Malignant tumors due to their characteristically larger nuclear size

Observations on concavity_mean¶

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(data, "concavity_mean", kde=True)

Observations:¶

  • Positively Skewed Distribution: The concavity_mean feature exhibits a significant right-skew (positive skewness of approximately 1.39), with the majority of samples clustered near zero and a long tail extending toward higher values.

  • Class Separation: There is a distinct difference in the severity of concave portions between diagnosis types, as malignant (M) samples consistently display higher concavity_mean values and greater variance compared to the low, tightly clustered values of benign (B) samples.

  • High Predictive Potential: Because higher concavity_mean values—representing more complex and irregular nuclear contours—are strongly correlated with malignancy, this feature serves as a critical morphological indicator for distinguishing between benign and cancerous breast masses.

Observations on radius_worst¶

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(data, "radius_worst", kde=True)

Observations:¶

  • The distribution is right-skewed (positively skewed), as evidenced by the long tail extending toward higher values and the mean (green dashed line/triangle) sitting to the right of the median (solid black line).

  • The box plot highlights a significant presence of outliers on the upper end, with several data points extending beyond the right whisker past the value of 30.

  • The radius_worst feature shows high variability and a clear tendency toward larger values in a subset of the data, which typically serves as a strong indicator for distinguishing malignant cases in this dataset.

Observations on area_worst¶

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(data, "area_worst", kde=True)

Observations:¶

  • The area_worst feature displays a highly right-skewed distribution, with most data points concentrated between 500 and 1,500 $units^2$, while a long tail of extreme values extends toward 4,000 $units^2$.

  • There is a stark contrast between classes, as Malignant (M) tumors exhibit significantly larger and more varied area_worst values compared to Benign (B) tumors, which are tightly grouped at the lower end of the scale.

  • Due to the minimal overlap between diagnostic groups, area_worst is one of the most powerful predictors in the dataset, where values exceeding roughly 1,000 $units^2$ serve as a highly reliable indicator for classifying a tumor as Malignant.

Observations on concave_points_worst¶

In [ ]:
histogram_boxplot(data, "concave_points_worst", kde=True)

Observations:¶

  • The concave_points_worst feature exhibits a bimodal distribution, characterized by a large cluster of samples near zero and another peak around the 0.15 mark.

  • While benign (B) samples are heavily concentrated at the lower end of the scale (below 0.1), malignant (M) samples show a much wider and higher range of values, often peaking between 0.15 and 0.25.

  • Strong Diagnostic Discriminator: Because there is a stark separation between the two classes with very little overlap, concave_points_worst is one of the most reliable features for predicting malignancy; values above 0.1 serve as a high-confidence indicator of cancerous tissue.

Final Conclusion – Univariate Analysis¶

  • The dataset exhibits a moderate class imbalance, with benign cases comprising approximately 63% of the samples, which should be considered during model evaluation.
  • For the remaining numeric features, summary statistics and unique value analysis confirm that all variables are continuous, contain complete observations, and show sufficient variability, indicating good overall data quality.
  • Several remaining features, particularly standard error and shape-related variables, display wide ranges and high maximum values, suggesting the presence of outliers but no near-zero variance features.
  • Key tumor size–related features (radius_mean, area_mean, radius_worst, and area_worst) show strong right-skewness and higher dispersion among malignant tumors.
  • Shape-related features (concavity_mean and concave_points_worst) demonstrate clear separation between benign and malignant classes.
  • Worst-case measurements consistently show higher means and wider ranges than corresponding mean features, reinforcing their predictive importance.
  • Overall, both the selected features and the remaining features analyzed through summary statistics are informative and suitable for downstream bivariate analysis and predictive modeling.

Bivariate Analysis¶

  • I have already completed univariate analysis. Since the dataset has 30 numeric features, I divided them into important and less important features.

  • For bivariate analysis, my goal is to understand how each important numeric feature behaves with respect to the diagnosis (Benign vs Malignant).

  • I will mainly use distribution plots and boxplots to visually compare both classes and identify features that clearly separate malignant and benign tumors.

  • For less important features, I will do a light review only to confirm they do not add much new information.

In [ ]:
# function to plot stacked bar chart


def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
    """
    Print the category counts and plot a stacked bar chart

    data: dataframe
    predictor: independent variable
    target: target variable
    """
    count = data[predictor].nunique()
    sorter = data[target].value_counts().index[-1]
    tab1 = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], margins=True).sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    print(tab1)
    print("-" * 120)
    tab = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], normalize="index").sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    tab.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, figsize=(count + 1, 5))
    plt.legend(
        loc="lower left",
        frameon=False,
    )
    plt.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1))
    plt.show()
In [ ]:
target = "Diagnosis"
In [ ]:
# Important Features

important_features = [
    "radius_mean",
    "texture_mean",
    "perimeter_mean",
    "area_mean",
    "concavity_mean",
    "concave points_mean",
    "radius_worst",
    "perimeter_worst",
    "area_worst"
]

These features were selected based on strong univariate patterns and known clinical relevance.¶

In [ ]:
df.head()
Out[ ]:

1 M 20.570 17.770 132.900 1326.000 0.085 0.079 0.087 0.070 0.181 0.057 0.543 0.734 3.398 74.080 0.005 0.013 0.019 0.013 0.014 0.004 24.990 23.410 158.800 1956.000 0.124 0.187 0.242 0.186 0.275 0.089
2 M 19.690 21.250 130.000 1203.000 0.110 0.160 0.197 0.128 0.207 0.060 0.746 0.787 4.585 94.030 0.006 0.040 0.038 0.021 0.022 0.005 23.570 25.530 152.500 1709.000 0.144 0.424 0.450 0.243 0.361 0.088
3 M 11.420 20.380 77.580 386.100 0.142 0.284 0.241 0.105 0.260 0.097 0.496 1.156 3.445 27.230 0.009 0.075 0.057 0.019 0.060 0.009 14.910 26.500 98.870 567.700 0.210 0.866 0.687 0.258 0.664 0.173
4 M 20.290 14.340 135.100 1297.000 0.100 0.133 0.198 0.104 0.181 0.059 0.757 0.781 5.438 94.440 0.011 0.025 0.057 0.019 0.018 0.005 22.540 16.670 152.200 1575.000 0.137 0.205 0.400 0.163 0.236 0.077

Observations on Diagnosis vs radius_mean¶

In [ ]:
# Plot distribution and boxplots of radius_mean with respect to diagnosis
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="radius_mean",
    target=target
)

Observations:¶

  • Distinct Central Tendencies: There is a significant difference in the average values between the two groups. The median radius_mean for malignant tumors (approx. 17.5) is notably higher than that for benign tumors (approx. 12.2). This suggests that, on average, malignant tumors are larger.

  • Separation and Overlap: While the two distributions overlap (specifically between the values of 11 and 16), the histograms show that malignant cases are skewed toward the right (higher values), while benign cases are concentrated toward the left. This indicates that radius_mean is a strong discriminatory feature for classification.

  • Variance and Outliers: The malignant group (green boxplot) shows a wider interquartile range and more spread than the benign group (blue boxplot). Additionally, the malignant distribution exhibits a "long tail" toward the higher end (up to 28+), while the benign distribution is more compact and follows a more symmetric, normal distribution curve.

Conclusion The feature radius_mean is a statistically significant predictor for breast cancer diagnosis. Because malignant tumors tend to have a substantially larger mean radius than benign ones with relatively clear separation in the boxplots, this variable should be prioritized as a primary input for any predictive machine learning model.

Observations on Diagnosis vs texture_mean¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="texture_mean",
    target=target
)

Observations:¶

  • Higher Average Values for Malignancy: Malignant tumors (M) generally exhibit higher texture_mean values, with a median around 21, compared to Benign tumors (B) which have a median around 17.
  • Significant Distribution Overlap: There is a substantial overlap between the two classes across the 15 to 25 range, indicating that texture alone may lead to a higher rate of false positives or negatives if used in isolation.
  • Presence of Outliers: Both groups show several high-value outliers (above 30 for malignant and above 26 for benign), which are more clearly visible in the "Boxplot w.r.t target" before they are filtered in the adjacent chart.
  • Distribution Shape: The malignant distribution appears roughly normal but with a slight right skew, while the benign distribution shows a more pronounced right tail with multiple small peaks in the higher texture ranges.

Conclusion¶

The texture_mean feature is a useful but secondary predictor for breast cancer diagnosis. While it shows a statistically visible difference in central tendency between classes, the moderate overlap suggests it is a weaker classifier than the radius_mean feature previously analyzed. It should be used in combination with size-related features to improve the overall accuracy of the classification model.

Observations on Diagnosis vs perimeter_mean¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="perimeter_mean",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Higher Mean Values for Malignant Tumors: The median perimeter_mean for malignant tumors is significantly higher (approximately 115.4) compared to benign tumors (approximately 78.0).
  • Low Overlap at Extremes: While there is some overlap in the mid-range (80–100), the distributions separate clearly at lower and higher values; benign tumors rarely exceed a perimeter of 110, whereas most malignant cases sit above this threshold.
  • High Predictability: Because perimeter is geometrically tied to radius and area, it shows a similarly strong ability to distinguish between classes, making it one of the top five most relevant features for diagnosis.

Conclusion¶

The perimeter_mean is a primary diagnostic indicator for malignancy. Due to the minimal overlap between the two classes compared to texture or smoothness features, a higher perimeter value is strongly correlated with a malignant diagnosis.

Observations on Diagnosis vs area_mean¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="area_mean",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Significant Difference in Averages: The median area_mean for malignant tumors is substantially higher (approximately 978.4) than that for benign tumors (approximately 462.8).
  • High Variance in Malignant Cases: Malignant tumors exhibit a much wider range of values, reaching a maximum of 2501.0, while benign tumors are more tightly clustered with a maximum of only 992.1.
  • Right-Skewed Distribution: The distribution for malignant cases is heavily right-skewed, showing a "long tail" of very large tumors, whereas the benign distribution is more compact and centered at lower values.

Conclusion¶

The area_mean is a highly effective discriminatory feature for breast cancer classification. Due to the clear separation in the boxplots and the significant difference in central tendencies, a high area_mean value is a strong indicator of malignancy and should be considered a primary feature for predictive modeling.

Observations on Diagnosis vs concavity_mean¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="concavity_mean",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Significant Difference in Concavity: Malignant tumors generally show much higher concavity_mean values, reflecting a higher severity of concave portions in their contours compared to benign tumors.
  • Low Overlap at Extreme Values: While both distributions are concentrated near zero, the malignant class has a much longer "tail" of high concavity values, whereas benign tumors rarely show high concavity.
  • High Correlation with Size Features: This feature is strongly correlated with other shape-related metrics like compactness_mean and concave points_mean, often being used alongside them to identify cancerous cells.

Conclusion¶

The concavity_mean is a strong discriminatory feature for breast cancer diagnosis. Because malignant nuclei typically exhibit more irregular and concave contours than the smoother, more uniform nuclei of benign cases, a higher concavity value is a reliable indicator of malignancy.

Observations on Diagnosis vs concave points_mean¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="concave_points_mean",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Significant Disparity in Mean Values: Malignant tumors exhibit a substantially higher average number of concave points (approximately 3.4x higher) compared to benign tumors.
  • Clear Class Separation: Unlike texture or symmetry, concave points_mean shows very little overlap between the two classes; benign tumors are heavily clustered near zero, while malignant cases are distributed toward significantly higher values.
  • High Importance for Feature Selection: This feature is consistently identified as one of the most important predictors in machine learning models (like Random Forest), often outperforming general concavity and other shape-related metrics.

Conclusion¶

The concave points_mean is a critical diagnostic feature for distinguishing between tumor types. Because it provides high statistical separation with minimal overlap, a higher count of concave portions in the cell nucleus contour is a very strong and reliable indicator of malignancy.

Observations on Diagnosis vs radius_worst¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="radius_worst",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Significant Separation in Values: The median radius_worst for malignant tumors is notably higher (approximately 21) compared to benign tumors (approximately 13.5).
  • Clear Thresholding Potential: There is minimal overlap between the two classes; the majority of benign cases fall below a radius_worst of 17, while the bulk of malignant cases sit above this value.
  • Presence of Outliers: Malignant cases show several extreme outliers reaching up to 36, indicating that "worst" case scenarios for tumor size are much more aggressive in malignancy.

Conclusion¶

The radius_worst is an exceptionally strong predictor for breast cancer diagnosis. Because the "worst" (largest) radius recorded for a tumor shows even clearer separation between classes than the "mean" radius, it serves as a highly reliable metric for identifying malignant cases in a clinical setting.

Observations on Diagnosis vs area_worst¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="area_worst",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Massive Disparity in Area: The median area_worst for malignant tumors is significantly higher—often more than double—compared to benign tumors.
  • Extreme Outliers in Malignant Cases: While the benign distribution is compact and mostly stays below 1,000, the malignant group shows a very large spread with several extreme cases reaching up to 4,254, indicating that the largest recorded areas are almost exclusively associated with malignancy.
  • Highly Distinct Distributions: There is very little overlap between the two classes in the histograms; the benign cases are heavily clustered at the lower end of the scale, whereas the malignant cases form a much wider, right-skewed distribution that shifts toward higher values.

Conclusion¶

The area_worst is one of the most powerful predictors in the entire dataset for distinguishing between tumor types. Due to the dramatic separation between the groups and the fact that extremely high area values are unique to the malignant class, this feature provides high confidence for classification and should be a top priority in model training.

Observations on Diagnosis vs fractal_dimension_mean¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(
    data=df,
    predictor="fractal_dimension_mean",
    target=target
)

Observation¶

  • Minimal Difference in Central Tendencies: The median values for both malignant (M) and benign (B) tumors are nearly identical, sitting very close to 0.061.
  • Significant Distribution Overlap: There is an almost complete overlap between the two classes across the entire range of values, as seen in both the histograms and the boxplots.
  • Lack of Predictive Power: Unlike size-related features (like area or radius), fractal_dimension_mean does not show a clear shift in distribution between the two diagnosis groups.
  • Presence of Outliers: Both groups exhibit several high-value outliers reaching above 0.08, but these are present in both benign and malignant cases, further complicating any attempt to use this feature for separation.

Conclusion¶

The fractal_dimension_mean is a weak predictor for breast cancer diagnosis in this dataset. Because there is no significant statistical separation between the malignant and benign distributions, this feature provides very little discriminatory information and would likely be ranked low in terms of feature importance for a classification model.

Handling of Remaining Features¶

  • The remaining features were reviewed using summary statistics and correlation analysis. These features showed patterns similar to the already analyzed important features or had weaker separation between malignant and benign tumors. To avoid redundancy, detailed bivariate plots were not generated for all remaining features.
In [ ]:
# Quick Statistical Check

# remaining features
analyzed_features = [
    "radius_mean", "texture_mean", "perimeter_mean", "area_mean",
    "concavity_mean", "concave points_mean",
    "radius_worst", "area_worst"
]

remaining_features = [f for f in df.columns if f not in analyzed_features + [target]]
remaining_features

Out[ ]:
Diagnosis B M
smoothness_mean 0.092 0.103
compactness_mean 0.080 0.145
concave_points_mean 0.026 0.088

fractal_dimension_mean 0.063 0.063
radius_se 0.284 0.609
texture_se 1.220 1.211
perimeter_se 2.000 4.324
area_se 21.135 72.672
smoothness_se 0.007 0.007
compactness_se 0.021 0.032
concavity_se 0.026 0.042
concave_points_se 0.010 0.015
symmetry_se 0.021 0.020
fractal_dimension_se 0.004 0.004
texture_worst 23.515 29.318
perimeter_worst 87.006 141.370

compactness_worst 0.183 0.375
concavity_worst 0.166 0.451
concave_points_worst 0.074 0.182
